label: Honeycomb
description: Connect to Honeycomb to query traces, events, and high-cardinality observability data from your datasets.

form.title: Connect Honeycomb
form.subtitle: Paste your Honeycomb API key to start querying traces and observability data.

methods.label: API Key
methods.description: Paste a Honeycomb API key generated from Account → Team Settings → API Keys. Quickest path — no OAuth client setup required.
methods.how_to:
## Generate a Honeycomb API Key

1. Log in to [Honeycomb](https://ui.honeycomb.io) and click your team name in the top-left corner.
2. Go to **Account → Team Settings → API Keys**.
3. Click **Create API Key**.
4. Give it a name like `Anton` so you can identify it later.
5. Select the permissions your use case needs — for read-only querying, enable **Query** access. Enable **Send Events** only if you want Anton to ingest data too.
6. Choose whether this key should be scoped to specific environments or all environments.
7. Click **Create** and copy the key immediately — it is shown only once.
8. Paste the key into the **API Key** field below.

## Notes

- Keys are environment-scoped by default in Honeycomb's newer UI. If you need access across multiple environments, create one key per environment or use a Configuration key.
- If you lose the key, generate a new one from the same page — Anton can swap it without further steps.
- See [Honeycomb API key docs](https://docs.honeycomb.io/get-started/configure/environments-and-api-keys/) for full details on key types and scopes.

fields.name: api_key
fields.label: API Key
fields.description: Found under Account → Team Settings → API Keys. The key is shown only once at creation time.

fields.name: environment
fields.label: Environment Name
fields.description: Optional. Specify the Honeycomb environment this key is scoped to (e.g. production, staging). Leave blank if your key has access to all environments.

fields.name: api_url
fields.label: API Base URL
fields.description: Override only if you are using Honeycomb EU (https://api.eu1.honeycomb.io) or a self-hosted instance.
